title: Covering up the Eleventh Angel
wikitext:
[]: Covering up the Eleventh Angel.             Quote Post by universalperson » Wed Apr 09, 2008 2:48 pm It's probably likely that the answers to these question will be "Fanwank something" But I'm intrested in the members thought processes. Why did Gendo try to hide the attack of the 11th Angel from SEELE, and furthermore, why didn't it seem to work, especially with Leliel being called the 12th Angel.

[]:             Quote Post by Dartz » Wed Apr 09, 2008 3:11 pm If you're trying to persuade an intransigent committee that, not only are you on their side and not actively intending to betray them, but also that your are competent in your job so that same commitee won't have a legitamate excuse to fire you, of course you're going to try and hide your 'setbacks' from time to time... Governments do it all the time... Just ask Bertie Ahern.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Apr 09, 2008 4:37 pm "I assure you, NERV is impenetrable." "What about these rumors of an 11th Ang-" "Never you mind, those are lies."

[]:             Quote Post by V » Wed Apr 09, 2008 4:46 pm Essentially, Gendo knew they'd flip at him for doing a bad job of letting an Angel get in that far (even though there's really nothing he could have done about it) As far as I can tell, Seele's reaction was that they realized Gendo was lying, to save face, but as the Angel was ultimately stopped, they were willing to let it slide with a harsh scolding. So yeah, Seele wasn't fooled, they just weren't willing to yell at Gendo more seeing as the Angel was defeated one way or another.

[]:             Quote Post by Semisubtle » Wed Apr 09, 2008 6:22 pm I agree, there was probably little point in getting Seele all in an uproar over an Angel that close to Nerv, but once it was defeated they couldn't really do much except yell based on principle.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Wed Apr 09, 2008 9:54 pm If your entire dream for the future was almost blown sky high I think you'd be just a little pissed off too. Ireul almost blew NERV up. Kiss Lilith and Eva-01 good-bye in a worst case scenario. I think you'd be questioning someone's competence at that point if you were in their shoes.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Sun Apr 20, 2008 4:09 pm I feel there are several reasons for this scene, and that it is really one of the classic moments in Evangelion where Anno packs information in as densely as possible. We learn among other things 1) Gendou's position is not entirely secure, and the committee has the power to remove him at any time. They hold the cards, at least officially. 2) The committee are essentially spying on Gendo(most likely using Kaji). And Gendou is perfectly prepared to lie and alter information in return. Their is a mutual mistrust at the table, which is strange in light of the obvious level of overt trust that the committee has placed in Gendou by making him commander. 3) Committee work is serious business. Gendou even faces execution if he is caught out in his lie. Moreover, Gendou is held personally accountable for all events that occur in the Geofront, even those outside his direct control. 4) The committee has a "schedule" of some kind, based on the Dead Sea Scrolls. They at least appear to know what they are doing, and are fairly anxious that their plans be carried out according to their own "scenario". 5) Keel is the boss. His final words on the matter override the rest of the groups questions and criticisms of Gendou, and also serve to put Gendou in his place. There may also be a hint here of a "special understanding" between Gendou and Keel, outside of the context of the committee. I love these scenes. Of all things, the committee reminds me most of the Syndicate from the X-files, with Keel as the Well Manicured Man . We always enter their conversations midway through, and they always seem to be discussing their own opaque plans and schedules, along with their cryptic details. We only understand one word in three, and can only tie things to the main plot in retrospect. But that is what makes such scenes so entertaining. They add a gentlemanly cloak and dagger element to the proceedings. Interestingly enough, such venerable old committees, who to outsiders seem to speak tersely and in riddles, and who reach collective agreement as if by telepathy, may in fact exist! Here's an excerpt from on of Richard Feynman's many anecdotes. Surely you're Joking Mr. Feynman! wrote: One of the first interesting experiences I had in this project at Princeton was meeting great men. I had never met very many great men before. But there was an evaluation committee that had to try to help us along, and help us ultimately decide which way we were going to separate the uranium. This committee had men like Compton and Tolman and Smyth and Urey and Rabi and Oppenheimer on it. I would sit in because I understood the theory of how our process of separating isotopes worked, and so they'd ask me questions and talk about it. In these discussions one man would make a point. Then Compton, for example, would explain a different point of view. He would say it should be this way, and he was perfectly right. Another guy would say, well, maybe, but there's this other possibility we have to consider against it. So everybody is disagreeing, all around the table. I am surprised and disturbed that Compton doesn't repeat and emphasize his point. Finally at the end, Tolman, who's the chairman, would say, "Well, having heard all these arguments, I guess it's true that Compton's argument is the best of all, and now we have to go ahead." It was such a shock to me to see that a committee of men could present a whole lot of ideas, each one thinking of a new facet, while remembering what the other fella said, so that, at the end, the decision is made as to which idea was the best-summing it all up-without having to say it three times. These were very great men indeed.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Mon Apr 21, 2008 5:20 am Well, to put is simply, I think most 'organisation' would try to cover up any major mistakes they have done to save face, and also, maybe to hide NERV's 'hidden' potential of its ability to go against SEELE...

[]:             Quote Post by sadsadshinji » Tue Apr 29, 2008 4:26 pm At the same time, the Eleventh Angel does seem somewhat suspicious; it was introduced into NERV three days before in a protein wall (Bardiel was introduced in a similar way, I suppose). I got the impression that Gendo was covering up so that everything stayed with the scenario, which could conceivable give him more time to complete his own plans.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Apr 29, 2008 7:13 pm A rather late reply to OMF's post, on Gendo being at the Committee's mercy- The committee, despite all its power, does not officially remove Gendo although it could have done so at any time. A very considerable period of time passed between Episode 14 and the end, and considering Gendo's increasing treachery, they could have removed him or killed him, but they did not. Despite their distrust, they need him, and Keel says so explicitly. Case in point, Seele targets people Gendo has used to further his own ends- Kaji and Ritsuko- rather than going direct for Gendo himself. The Committee was bound to find out about Ireul sooner or later. Why did Gendo continue to lie? Because he didn't want them to take over and try eradicating the Angel themselves. If Seele heard of the intrusion and the Magi being taken over, they would have likely dropped N2 mines and blown the whole Geofront to kingdom come rather than let Ireul win. And rather than tell them cold turkey that he lied (And create a very awkward situation in a Committee meeting), Gendo let them find out about his lie themselves. And as for Feynman- well, that man had his brains where they mattered the most. Pity the world doesn't have geniuses like him any longer.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Apr 30, 2008 5:25 am So, eitherway, it goes according to GENDO's plan, being the master manipulator.

[]: Covering up the Eleventh Angel.             Quote Post by universalperson » Wed Apr 09, 2008 2:48 pm It's probably likely that the answers to these question will be "Fanwank something" But I'm intrested in the members thought processes. Why did Gendo try to hide the attack of the 11th Angel from SEELE, and furthermore, why didn't it seem to work, especially with Leliel being called the 12th Angel.

Article: Covering up the Eleventh Angel
 I love these scenes. Of all things, the committee reminds me most of the Syndicate from the X-files, with Keel as the Well Manicured Man . We always enter their conversations midway through, and they always seem to be discussing their own opaque plans and schedules, along with their cryptic details. We only understand one word in three, and can only tie things to the main plot in retrospect. But that is what makes such scenes so entertaining. They add a gentlemanly cloak and dagger element to the proceedings. Interestingly enough, such venerable old committees, who to outsiders seem to speak tersely and in riddles, and who reach collective agreement as if by telepathy, may in fact exist! Here's an excerpt from on of Richard Feynman's many anecdotes. Surely you're Joking Mr. Feynman! wrote: One of the first interesting experiences I had in this project at Princeton was meeting great men. I had never met very many great men before. But there was an evaluation committee that had to try to help us along, and help us ultimately decide which way we were going to separate the uranium. This committee had men like Compton and Tolman and Smyth and Urey and Rabi and Oppenheimer on it. I would sit in because I understood the theory of how our process of separating isotopes worked, and so they'd ask me questions and talk about it. In these discussions one man would make a point. Then Compton, for example, would explain a different point of view. He would say it should be this way, and he was perfectly right. Another guy would say, well, maybe, but there's this other possibility we have to consider against it. So everybody is disagreeing, all around the table. I am surprised and disturbed that Compton doesn't repeat and emphasize his point. Finally at the end, Tolman, who's the chairman, would say, "Well, having heard all these arguments, I guess it's true that Compton's argument is the best of all, and now we have to go ahead." It was such a shock to me to see that a committee of men could present a whole lot of ideas, each one thinking of a new facet, while remembering what the other fella said, so that, at the end, the decision is made as to which idea was the best-summing it all up-without having to say it three times. These were very great men indeed.
